answer:
Dark Mode — Debug Scenario

Core Definition

Dark mode is an alternate color scheme that uses light-colored text and UI elements on dark backgrounds. Implementation typically involves CSS custom properties, a theme toggle mechanism, and persistence of user preference across sessions.

Manoj's Project Context

In the CRM Sales Dashboard, Manoj debugged a dark mode issue where the sales charts had white backgrounds that created a blinding flash when toggling to dark mode. The chart library (Chart.js) wasn't respecting CSS custom properties, requiring explicit theme-aware configuration.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

/* Dark mode CSS with custom properties */
:root {
  --bg-primary: #ffffff;
  --bg-secondary: #f8fafc;
  --text-primary: #1e293b;
  --text-secondary: #64748b;
  --border-color: #e2e8f0;
  --chart-bg: #ffffff;
  --chart-grid: #e2e8f0;
}

[data-theme='dark'] {
  --bg-primary: #0f172a;
  --bg-secondary: #1e293b;
  --text-primary: #f1f5f9;
  --text-secondary: #94a3b8;
  --border-color: #334155;
  --chart-bg: #1e293b;
  --chart-grid: #334155;
}

/* Theme toggle with persistence */
function toggleTheme() {
  const current = document.documentElement.getAttribute('data-theme');
  const next = current === 'dark' ? 'light' : 'dark';
  document.documentElement.setAttribute('data-theme', next);
  localStorage.setItem('theme', next);

  // Bug fix: Chart.js doesn't observe CSS variables
  // Must explicitly update chart instances
  document.querySelectorAll('canvas').forEach(canvas => {
    const chart = Chart.getChart(canvas);
    if (chart) {
      const styles = getComputedStyle(document.documentElement);
      chart.options.scales.x.grid.color = styles.getPropertyValue('--chart-grid');
      chart.options.scales.y.grid.color = styles.getPropertyValue('--chart-grid');
      chart.options.scales.x.ticks.color = styles.getPropertyValue('--text-secondary');
      chart.options.scales.y.ticks.color = styles.getPropertyValue('--text-secondary');
      chart.options.plugins.legend.labels.color = styles.getPropertyValue('--text-primary');
      chart.canvas.parentElement.style.backgroundColor = styles.getPropertyValue('--chart-bg');
      chart.update('none'); // 'none' skips animation for instant swap
    }
  });
}

// Initialize: respect system preference, then localStorage override
const saved = localStorage.getItem('theme');
const prefersDark = window.matchMedia('(prefers-color-scheme: dark)').matches;
document.documentElement.setAttribute('data-theme', saved ?? (prefersDark ? 'dark' : 'light'));

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

On the CRM Dashboard, users reported a "flash of white" on charts when switching to dark mode. Manoj discovered that Chart.js renders to a canvas element that doesn't inherit CSS custom properties — it uses JavaScript configuration objects for colors. He created a `syncChartTheme()` function that reads computed CSS variable values and pushes them into every Chart.js instance via `chart.update('none')`. He also found that the chart background needed explicit styling on the parent container since canvas itself is transparent. The fix eliminated the white flash entirely.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Canvas-based libraries ignore CSS variablesSync theme changes programmatically via library API
Flash of unstyled content on page loadSet theme attribute in a blocking script in <head>
System preference changes ignored after manual toggleUse matchMedia listener but respect localStorage override

How to Validate

  • Toggle dark mode and verify all charts update without white flash
  • Refresh the page and confirm theme persists from localStorage
  • Change OS dark mode setting and verify app follows (if no manual override)
  • Test with Playwright: screenshot comparison in both themes

Dark Mode — Compare Two Approaches

Core Definition

Two common dark mode implementation strategies are: CSS custom properties with a data attribute (toggle a `data-theme` attribute that swaps variable values) versus Tailwind CSS dark variant (use `dark:` prefix classes controlled by a class-based or media-based strategy).

Manoj's Project Context

For ContactsHub (Tailwind + shadcn/ui), Manoj evaluated both approaches. Since shadcn/ui already provides a theming system built on CSS variables and the `dark` class, he chose the Tailwind dark variant approach to stay consistent with the component library's conventions.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

/* Approach A — CSS Custom Properties */
:root {
  --bg-primary: #ffffff;
  --text-primary: #1e293b;
  --card-bg: #f8fafc;
}
[data-theme='dark'] {
  --bg-primary: #0f172a;
  --text-primary: #f1f5f9;
  --card-bg: #1e293b;
}
.card { background: var(--card-bg); color: var(--text-primary); }

/* Approach B — Tailwind dark variant (ContactsHub choice) */
// tailwind.config.ts
export default {
  darkMode: 'class', // or 'selector' in Tailwind v4
  // ...
}

// Component using dark: variant
function ContactCard({ contact }) {
  return (
    <div className="bg-white dark:bg-slate-800 text-slate-900 dark:text-slate-100
                    border border-slate-200 dark:border-slate-700 rounded-lg p-4">
      <h3 className="font-semibold">{contact.name}</h3>
      <p className="text-slate-500 dark:text-slate-400">{contact.email}</p>
    </div>
  );
}

// Theme toggle (works with shadcn/ui)
function ThemeToggle() {
  const [dark, setDark] = useState(
    () => document.documentElement.classList.contains('dark')
  );

  function toggle() {
    document.documentElement.classList.toggle('dark');
    const isDark = document.documentElement.classList.contains('dark');
    setDark(isDark);
    localStorage.setItem('theme', isDark ? 'dark' : 'light');
  }

  return <button onClick={toggle}>{dark ? '☀️' : '🌙'}</button>;
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, Manoj initially used CSS custom properties but ran into friction with shadcn/ui components that expected Tailwind's `dark:` class convention. He migrated to `darkMode: 'class'` in Tailwind config, which aligned with shadcn/ui's theme provider. The shadcn/ui components automatically adjusted when the `dark` class was present on ``. For custom components, Manoj added `dark:` variants. The key lesson: choose the approach that matches your component library's expectations.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Mixing both approaches creates inconsistencyPick one strategy and use it throughout; shadcn/ui uses CSS variables under the hood with Tailwind's class strategy
FOUC (flash of unstyled content) on page loadAdd inline script in <head> to set dark class before render
Third-party components don't respect your themeOverride their styles using CSS variable values or wrapper components

How to Validate

  • Toggle theme and verify all components (including shadcn/ui) update
  • Reload page — confirm no flash of wrong theme
  • Test with system dark mode preference and manual override
  • Run Playwright visual regression tests in both themes

Animation Performance — Debug Scenario

Core Definition

Animation performance refers to achieving smooth 60fps animations by leveraging GPU-accelerated CSS properties (transform, opacity), avoiding layout thrashing, and using the browser's compositor thread effectively. Poor animation performance causes visible jank and degraded user experience.

Manoj's Project Context

In the CRM Sales Dashboard, Manoj debugged a janky sidebar slide-in animation that dropped to 15fps on mid-range devices. The animation was transitioning `width` and `left` properties instead of using GPU-accelerated `transform: translateX()`.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

/* BAD: Layout-triggering animation */
.sidebar-bad {
  position: fixed;
  left: -300px;
  width: 0;
  transition: left 0.3s ease, width 0.3s ease; /* triggers layout each frame */
}
.sidebar-bad.open {
  left: 0;
  width: 300px;
}

/* GOOD: GPU-accelerated animation */
.sidebar {
  position: fixed;
  left: 0;
  width: 300px;
  transform: translateX(-100%); /* off-screen via transform */
  transition: transform 0.3s cubic-bezier(0.4, 0, 0.2, 1);
  will-change: transform; /* hint for compositor layer */
}
.sidebar.open {
  transform: translateX(0);
}

/* Backdrop fade — opacity is also compositor-friendly */
.sidebar-backdrop {
  position: fixed;
  inset: 0;
  background: rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.5);
  opacity: 0;
  transition: opacity 0.3s ease;
  pointer-events: none;
}
.sidebar-backdrop.visible {
  opacity: 1;
  pointer-events: auto;
}

/* React component */
function Sidebar({ isOpen, onClose }) {
  return (
    <>
      <div className={`sidebar-backdrop ${isOpen ? 'visible' : ''}`} onClick={onClose} />
      <aside className={`sidebar ${isOpen ? 'open' : ''}`}>
        <nav>{/* navigation items */}</nav>
      </aside>
    </>
  );
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

Users on tablets reported the CRM Dashboard sidebar animation was "choppy." Manoj opened Chrome DevTools Performance panel and recorded the animation — it showed constant Layout and Paint events at 15fps. The culprit: `transition: width 0.3s, left 0.3s` which triggers layout recalculation on every frame. He refactored to use `transform: translateX()` with a fixed width sidebar, and added `will-change: transform` to promote the element to its own compositor layer. Post-fix, the Performance panel showed only Composite events at a solid 60fps. He also replaced `requestAnimationFrame` loops with CSS transitions for simple state changes.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Animating width/height/top/left triggers layoutUse transform (translate, scale) and opacity only
will-change on too many elements wastes GPU memoryApply will-change only during animation, remove after
JavaScript animations block main threadUse CSS transitions or Web Animations API for simple animations

How to Validate

  • Record animation in Chrome DevTools Performance panel — no Layout/Paint events
  • Check Layers panel to confirm animated element has its own compositor layer
  • Test on low-end device or with CPU throttling (4x slowdown)
  • Verify 60fps in the FPS meter during animation

Animation Performance — Compare Two Approaches

Core Definition

Two approaches to web animations: CSS Transitions/Animations (declarative, compositor-friendly, limited control) versus JavaScript-driven animations (Web Animations API or requestAnimationFrame, imperative, more flexible but requires careful optimization). The choice depends on animation complexity and interactivity requirements.

Manoj's Project Context

In ContactsHub, Manoj compared CSS transitions for simple hover effects and page transitions versus JavaScript-driven animations for a drag-and-drop contact reordering feature that needed dynamic positioning based on pointer location.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

/* Approach A — CSS Transition (ContactsHub page transitions) */
.contact-card {
  transition: transform 0.2s ease, box-shadow 0.2s ease;
}
.contact-card:hover {
  transform: translateY(-2px);
  box-shadow: 0 8px 25px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15);
}

/* Page enter animation */
.page-enter {
  opacity: 0;
  transform: translateY(10px);
}
.page-enter-active {
  opacity: 1;
  transform: translateY(0);
  transition: opacity 0.3s ease, transform 0.3s ease;
}

// Approach B — Web Animations API (drag-and-drop reorder)
function handleDragMove(event: PointerEvent, draggedEl: HTMLElement, items: HTMLElement[]) {
  const y = event.clientY;

  // Animate dragged element to follow pointer
  draggedEl.animate(
    [{ transform: `translateY(${y - draggedEl.offsetTop}px)` }],
    { duration: 0, fill: 'forwards' }
  );

  // Animate other items to make space
  items.forEach((item, i) => {
    const rect = item.getBoundingClientRect();
    const offset = y > rect.top + rect.height / 2 ? 60 : 0;

    item.animate(
      [{ transform: `translateY(${offset}px)` }],
      { duration: 200, fill: 'forwards', easing: 'cubic-bezier(0.2, 0, 0, 1)' }
    );
  });
}

// Best practice: CSS for simple, JS for interactive
function ContactList() {
  const handlePointerDown = (e) => { /* start drag with WAAPI */ };
  return (
    <div>
      {contacts.map(c => (
        <div
          key={c.id}
          className="contact-card" /* CSS hover transitions */
          onPointerDown={handlePointerDown} /* JS drag animations */
        >{c.name}</div>
      ))}
    </div>
  );
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, Manoj initially used CSS transitions for drag-and-drop reordering, but the animation was stepped rather than smooth because CSS transitions require discrete class toggles. He switched to the Web Animations API for the drag interaction, using `element.animate()` with zero-duration keyframes for instant position updates during drag, and 200ms easing for neighboring items shifting. Simple hover effects and page transitions remained as CSS transitions. This hybrid approach gave smooth drag interactions while keeping static animations lightweight.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Using JS animations for simple hover/toggle effectsCSS transitions are simpler and more performant for state-based animations
WAAPI animations not cleaned upCall animation.cancel() on unmount to prevent memory leaks
requestAnimationFrame without throttlingUse WAAPI instead; if rAF needed, batch DOM reads and writes

How to Validate

  • Profile drag interaction in Performance panel — smooth 60fps
  • Verify hover transitions run on compositor (no Layout events)
  • Test on mobile device with 4x CPU throttle
  • Check for animation memory leaks on repeated drag operations

Design System — Compare Two Approaches

Core Definition

A design system is a collection of reusable components, design tokens, and guidelines that ensure visual and behavioral consistency across an application. Two approaches: building from scratch (full control, high effort) versus adopting an existing system like shadcn/ui (fast start, opinionated but customizable).

Manoj's Project Context

For ContactsHub, Manoj evaluated building custom components versus adopting shadcn/ui with Tailwind. He chose shadcn/ui because it provides accessible, unstyled primitives (built on Radix UI) that he could customize with Tailwind classes, saving weeks of development while maintaining full control over styling.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

// Approach A — Custom design system component
interface ButtonProps extends React.ButtonHTMLAttributes<HTMLButtonElement> {
  variant: 'primary' | 'secondary' | 'danger';
  size: 'sm' | 'md' | 'lg';
}

const styles = {
  primary: 'bg-blue-600 text-white hover:bg-blue-700',
  secondary: 'bg-gray-100 text-gray-900 hover:bg-gray-200',
  danger: 'bg-red-600 text-white hover:bg-red-700',
};
const sizes = { sm: 'px-3 py-1 text-sm', md: 'px-4 py-2', lg: 'px-6 py-3 text-lg' };

function Button({ variant, size, className, ...props }: ButtonProps) {
  return (
    <button
      className={`rounded-md font-medium transition-colors
        focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-offset-2
        ${styles[variant]} ${sizes[size]} ${className}`}
      {...props}
    />
  );
}

// Approach B — shadcn/ui (ContactsHub choice)
// npx shadcn-ui@latest add button
// components/ui/button.tsx — you OWN this code
import { cva, type VariantProps } from 'class-variance-authority';
import { Slot } from '@radix-ui/react-slot';

const buttonVariants = cva(
  'inline-flex items-center justify-center rounded-md text-sm font-medium transition-colors focus-visible:outline-none focus-visible:ring-2',
  {
    variants: {
      variant: {
        default: 'bg-primary text-primary-foreground hover:bg-primary/90',
        destructive: 'bg-destructive text-destructive-foreground hover:bg-destructive/90',
        outline: 'border border-input bg-background hover:bg-accent',
        ghost: 'hover:bg-accent hover:text-accent-foreground',
      },
      size: {
        sm: 'h-9 px-3',
        default: 'h-10 px-4 py-2',
        lg: 'h-11 px-8',
      },
    },
    defaultVariants: { variant: 'default', size: 'default' },
  }
);

export function Button({ className, variant, size, asChild, ...props }) {
  const Comp = asChild ? Slot : 'button';
  return <Comp className={buttonVariants({ variant, size, className })} {...props} />;
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, Manoj initially started building custom components but realized he was spending days on keyboard navigation and ARIA attributes for dialogs and dropdowns. After switching to shadcn/ui, he got accessible Dialog, Select, and Command components from Radix UI primitives — pre-built with proper focus management and screen reader support. He customized the theme by editing CSS variables in `globals.css` and adjusting component styles directly in the copied source files. The result: a fully accessible UI in days instead of weeks.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Custom components lack keyboard navigationUse Radix primitives as foundation for complex interactive components
Design tokens scattered across filesCentralize in CSS variables (shadcn uses globals.css)
shadcn updates not automatically appliedTrack upstream changelog; selectively update components you need

How to Validate

  • Run axe-core audit — all components should pass a11y checks
  • Tab through all interactive elements — verify focus management
  • Test with screen reader (NVDA/VoiceOver)
  • Verify theme customization applies consistently across all components

Design System — Design Implementation

Core Definition

Designing a design system implementation involves defining design tokens (colors, spacing, typography), creating composable UI components with consistent APIs, establishing patterns for theming and variants, and documenting usage guidelines for the development team.

Manoj's Project Context

For the HR Leave Management System, Manoj designed a component system using shadcn/ui as the base, extended with domain-specific components like LeaveCalendar, ApprovalCard, and BalanceWidget. He established design tokens for the HR brand colors and created a component catalog for the team.

Architecture Diagram

Code Example

/* Design tokens — globals.css */
@layer base {
  :root {
    --background: 0 0% 100%;
    --foreground: 222.2 84% 4.9%;
    --primary: 221.2 83.2% 53.3%;      /* HR brand blue */
    --primary-foreground: 210 40% 98%;
    --success: 142.1 76.2% 36.3%;      /* Approved green */
    --warning: 38 92% 50%;              /* Pending amber */
    --destructive: 0 84.2% 60.2%;      /* Rejected red */
    --radius: 0.5rem;
  }
}

// Domain component built on design system primitives
import { Card, CardContent, CardHeader, CardTitle } from '@/components/ui/card';
import { Badge } from '@/components/ui/badge';

interface LeaveCardProps {
  leave: {
    type: string;
    startDate: string;
    endDate: string;
    status: 'pending' | 'approved' | 'rejected';
    reason: string;
  };
}

const statusVariant = {
  pending: 'warning',
  approved: 'success',
  rejected: 'destructive',
} as const;

export function LeaveCard({ leave }: LeaveCardProps) {
  return (
    <Card>
      <CardHeader className="flex flex-row items-center justify-between">
        <CardTitle className="text-sm font-medium">{leave.type}</CardTitle>
        <Badge variant={statusVariant[leave.status]}>{leave.status}</Badge>
      </CardHeader>
      <CardContent>
        <p className="text-sm text-muted-foreground">
          {leave.startDate} — {leave.endDate}
        </p>
        <p className="text-sm mt-1">{leave.reason}</p>
      </CardContent>
    </Card>
  );
}

Manoj's Debugging Narrative

While building the HR Leave System, Manoj found that different developers were using inconsistent spacing and color values across pages. He centralized all design tokens in CSS variables and created domain components (LeaveCard, ApprovalCard, BalanceWidget) that compose shadcn/ui primitives. This ensured that the leave-status badge always uses the correct color variant — green for approved, amber for pending, red for rejected — regardless of which developer built the page. He also created a simple Storybook-like catalog page at `/dev/components` for the team to reference during development.

Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallMitigation
Developers bypass design system with inline stylesCode review rules: no arbitrary color/spacing values; use tokens
Too many component variantsStart minimal; add variants only when real use cases demand them
Design tokens not matching Figma designsGenerate tokens from Figma (or vice versa) with shared naming convention

How to Validate

  • Audit codebase for hard-coded color values — all should use CSS variables
  • Verify all status badges use correct variant mapping
  • Check component catalog page renders all variants correctly
  • Run visual regression tests on key component combinations
